section.title: A Chosen People
section.sectionRationale: Peter gathers up the new birth and enduring word of chapter one and shows what they build: a people. Coming to Christ the living stone, believers are joined as living stones into a spiritual house and given the great titles once borne by Israel. This passage is the identity charter of the church, defining who these scattered exiles are before God.
section.thesis: Coming to Christ the living stone, rejected by men but chosen and precious to God, believers are built into a spiritual house and made a chosen people, a royal priesthood, and a holy nation, called out of darkness to proclaim the praises of the God who has made them His own.
section.frameNote: Three movements: crave pure milk and grow up, verses 1 to 3; the living stone and the two responses to Him, verses 4 to 8; and you are a chosen people, verses 9 to 10.

units.label: Crave pure milk and grow
units.note: having tasted that the Lord is good

verses.text: Rid yourselves, therefore, of all malice, deceit, hypocrisy, envy, and slander.
Like newborn babies, crave pure spiritual {{gala|milk}}, so that by it you may grow up in your salvation,
now that you have tasted that the Lord is good.

units.label: The living stone and two responses
units.note: chosen and precious, or a stone of stumbling

verses.text: As you come to Him, the {{lithos|living stone}}, rejected by men but chosen and precious in God’s sight,
you also, like living stones, are being built into a spiritual house to be a holy priesthood, offering spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ.
For it stands in Scripture: “See, I lay in Zion a stone, a chosen and precious {{akrogoniaios|cornerstone}}; and the one who believes in Him will never be put to shame.”
To you who believe, then, this stone is precious. But to those who do not believe, “The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone,”
and, “A stone of stumbling and a rock of offense.” They stumble because they disobey the word, and to this they were appointed.

units.label: You are a chosen people
units.note: out of darkness into His marvelous light

verses.text: But you are a {{genos|chosen people}}, a {{hierateuma|royal priesthood}}, a holy nation, a {{laos|people for God’s own possession}}, to proclaim the virtues of Him who called you out of darkness into His marvelous light.
Once you were not a people, but now you are the people of God; once you had not received mercy, but now you have received mercy.

gala.gloss: Pure spiritual milk, the unadulterated nourishment for the soul, closely tied to the word of God just mentioned. As newborn babies crave milk, believers are to crave this pure nourishment so they grow up in salvation. It is wholesome and untainted, the opposite of the malice and deceit to be put away.

lithos.gloss: A living stone. Peter calls Christ the living stone, rejected by men but chosen and precious to God, and then calls believers living stones too. The image joins Christ, the risen and life-giving foundation, to His people, who share His life and are built together on Him.

akrogoniaios.gloss: The cornerstone, the chief stone that anchors and aligns the whole building. Quoting Isaiah, Peter says God lays in Zion a chosen and precious cornerstone. Christ is that stone: those who trust Him are never put to shame, while those who reject Him stumble over the very stone God has honored.

hierateuma.gloss: A royal priesthood. Drawn from Exodus 19, this title once given to Israel now belongs to the church. All believers together form a priesthood serving the King, offering spiritual sacrifices and mediating God's light to the world through Christ, the one great High Priest.

genos.gloss: A chosen race or people. Echoing Isaiah 43, it names the church as God's elect people, chosen by grace. What was said of Israel is applied to this mixed body of Jewish and Gentile believers, one new people belonging to God across every nation.

laos.gloss: A people for God's own possession, His treasured people. From Exodus 19 and Malachi 3, it marks the church as God's special possession, purchased and claimed for Himself. Once no people at all, they are now the people of God, made His own by mercy.

basic.context: Peter tells his readers what to put off and what to crave. Put off malice, deceit, hypocrisy, envy, and slander; crave pure spiritual milk, like a hungry newborn, so you grow. Then he shows the great picture: as you come to Jesus, the living stone whom people rejected but God chose and treasures, you are built together into a spiritual house, a holy priesthood. Those who trust Him are never put to shame; those who reject Him stumble over Him. And then the sweeping declaration: you, once nobodies, are now a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God's own treasured possession.
basic.hermeneutics: Peter piles up titles that God once gave Israel at Sinai and applies them to the church, a mixed body of Jewish and Gentile believers. This is not replacement so much as fulfilment: the promises find their yes in Christ and flow to all who belong to Him. The purpose clause matters too, they are chosen not merely to enjoy status but to proclaim the praises of God and shine His light.
basic.originalLanguages: Crave the pure logikon gala, spiritual milk, and grow. Come to Christ, the lithos zon, the living stone, the chosen and precious akrogoniaios, cornerstone. You are a genos eklekton, a chosen people, a basileion hierateuma, a royal priesthood, a laos eis peripoiesin, a people for God's own possession.
basic.typology: The stone prophecies from Isaiah and Psalm 118, the cornerstone, the stone of stumbling, the rejected stone become chief, all converge on Christ. The titles chosen people, royal priesthood, holy nation, and treasured possession are lifted straight from Exodus 19, where Israel was constituted at Sinai, now fulfilled in the church. The spiritual house and priesthood fulfil the temple and its sacrifices.
basic.christ: Christ is the living stone, the cornerstone God lays in Zion, rejected by the builders yet made the chief stone. Everything is built on Him. To trust Him is to be honored and never put to shame; to reject Him is to stumble and fall. He is both the foundation of the church and the dividing line of humanity.
basic.god: God is the one who chooses. He chose the stone that men rejected, and He chooses a people who were once no people. He calls them out of darkness into His marvelous light and makes them His own treasured possession. He is a God of sovereign, surprising grace who takes outsiders and makes them His holy nation.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 19 gives the titles kingdom of priests and holy nation. Isaiah 28 and Psalm 118 supply the cornerstone and rejected stone. Isaiah 8 gives the stone of stumbling. And Hosea speaks of those who were not a people becoming God's people, which Peter echoes in verse 10.
basic.oneStory: At Sinai God made Israel a kingdom of priests and a holy nation. Through the prophets He promised a chosen cornerstone in Zion and a day when outsiders would become His people. In Christ, the rejected yet chosen stone, all of it comes true: a new people, drawn from every nation, built into God's house to declare His praise. This is the church, the fulfilment of the long story of God gathering a people for Himself.

context.literary: The therefore of verse 1 flows from the new birth and enduring word of chapter one. Verses 1 to 3 use the image of infants craving milk; verses 4 to 8 develop the metaphor of stones and a building, weaving together three Old Testament stone texts; verses 9 to 10 climax with a cascade of covenant titles. The section moves from personal growth to corporate identity, from milk to the house of God.
context.historical: The readers were a small, often despised minority, treated as social nobodies. To be told they are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, and a holy nation would be startling and dignifying. The stone imagery, especially the rejected stone become cornerstone, mirrors their own experience of rejection while affirming that God's chosen ones are often the world's rejected ones.
context.canonical: This is one of the New Testament's densest concentrations of Old Testament citation, drawing on Isaiah, the Psalms, Exodus, and Hosea. It stands at the heart of the Bible's teaching on the church as the continuation and fulfilment of God's covenant people, and it grounds the priesthood of all believers, a truth of enormous later importance.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To define the identity of the scattered believers as God's chosen people built on Christ the living stone, to show that response to Christ divides all humanity, and to summon them to grow in grace and proclaim the praises of the God who called them out of darkness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The section blends both. Verses 1 to 2 prescribe: put off sin, crave the pure milk. But the weight falls on description: you are being built into a house, you are a chosen people. The identity is the ground; the conduct and mission, put off sin, grow up, proclaim His praises, flow from it.

debates.question: How do the church's titles relate to Israel, verse 9?

views.label: Fulfilment and expansion in Christ
views.case: The promises to Israel find their yes in Christ and overflow to all who are united to Him, Jew and Gentile alike. The church does not erase Israel but is the flowering of God's one covenant people around the Messiah.

views.label: The church as the true Israel now
views.case: Others read Peter as applying Israel's identity directly to the church, which now bears these titles as God's people. Either way, the titles belong to those united to Christ by faith.

debates.bearing: Both affirm that in Christ a people drawn from every nation now holds the covenant dignity once given at Sinai, and holds it for the mission of declaring God's praise.

debates.question: What does it mean that they stumble, to this they were appointed, verse 8?

views.label: Appointed to the consequence of unbelief
views.case: Those who disobey the word stumble over Christ, and God has appointed that stumbling is the certain outcome of rejecting Him. The appointment concerns the fixed result, not a decree that removes their responsibility.

views.label: A statement of God's sovereign ordering
views.case: Others hear a stronger note of divine sovereignty over even the unbelief of the rejecting, held together in Scripture with their real guilt for disobeying the word.

debates.bearing: Both readings keep two truths together: those who reject Christ are truly responsible for their disobedience, and their stumbling falls within God's sovereign ordering of all things. Peter offers this as sober warning, not speculation.

study.originalLanguages: Believers are to crave the pure logikon gala, spiritual milk, the wholesome nourishment of the word, and so grow up in salvation. Coming to Christ, the lithos zon, the living stone, they are built as living stones into a spiritual house. Isaiah's prophecy is fulfilled in Him, the chosen and precious akrogoniaios, the cornerstone; those who believe are never put to shame, while the disobedient stumble over the very stone God honored. Then comes the cascade of covenant titles from Exodus and Isaiah: a genos eklekton, a chosen race, a basileion hierateuma, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a laos eis peripoiesin, a people for God's own possession, called out of darkness into His marvelous light.

typology.type: The living stone and cornerstone in Zion
typology.fulfillment: Christ, foretold in Isaiah and Psalm 118 as the chosen cornerstone and the rejected stone become chief, the foundation of God's house.
Israel's calling at Sinai, Exodus 19:5 to 6, now fulfilled in the church, a priestly people offering spiritual sacrifices through Christ.

typology.type: Once not a people, now God's people
typology.fulfillment: Hosea's promise that those called not my people would be called children of the living God, realized in the church gathered by mercy.

study.christ: Christ is the living stone at the centre of the passage, rejected by men but chosen and precious to God, the cornerstone laid in Zion. The whole spiritual house is built on Him, and every believer is joined to Him as a living stone sharing His life. He is also the great divide: to those who believe He is precious and a sure foundation; to those who reject Him He is a stone of stumbling and a rock of offense. Union with Him turns nobodies into God's chosen people, and it is through Him that their spiritual sacrifices are acceptable to God.
study.god: God is revealed as the sovereign chooser and merciful gatherer of a people. He chose and treasured the very stone that men threw away, and He chooses a people who were once no people, calling them out of darkness into His marvelous light. He constitutes them a royal priesthood and a holy nation, His own possession, purely by mercy. He is a God who delights to take the rejected and the outsider and make them His treasured people for His praise.

crossReferences.note: Israel called a kingdom of priests and a holy nation, the covenant titles Peter applies to the church.
God lays in Zion a chosen and precious cornerstone; whoever believes will not be put to shame, quoted in verse 6.
The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone, applied to Christ in verse 7.
Those who were not my people will be called my people, echoed in verse 10 as mercy given to the church.

study.oneStory: God formed Israel at Sinai as a kingdom of priests and a holy nation, and through the prophets promised a chosen cornerstone in Zion and a day when outsiders would become His own. In Christ, the stone men rejected but God chose, all of it is fulfilled. A new people, drawn from every nation and once no people at all, is built into God's house and made His treasured possession, called out of darkness to proclaim His praise. Here the long story of God gathering a people reaches its bright fulfilment in the church.

quiz.q: What are believers to crave in order to grow up in salvation?

options.text: Pure spiritual milk, the wholesome nourishment of God's word.
options.note: Verse 2: like newborns, believers crave the pure milk and grow.

options.text: Deeper spiritual experiences beyond the basics.
options.note: Peter points to pure nourishment tied to the word, not to experiences.

options.text: Whatever teaching feels most impressive.
options.note: The milk is pure and unadulterated, contrasted with malice and deceit.

options.text: The good word of the Lord they have already tasted.
options.note: True, they have tasted the Lord is good, though the answer should name the pure milk to be craved.

quiz.q: Who is the living stone, and how is He described?

options.text: Christ, rejected by men but chosen and precious in God's sight.
options.note: Verse 4: the rejected yet chosen stone on whom the house is built.

options.text: Christ, the cornerstone laid in Zion foretold in Scripture.
options.note: True, from verse 6, though verse 4 stresses His rejection by men and choice by God.

options.text: Each believer, who is the foundation of the church.
options.note: Believers are living stones, but Christ alone is the foundation and cornerstone.

options.text: Peter himself as the rock of the church.
options.note: The stone here is Christ, on whom all believers are built.

quiz.q: What happens to those who do not believe in the stone?

options.text: They stumble over Him, for He becomes a stone of stumbling and rock of offense.
options.note: Verse 8: the same stone that saves believers becomes a stumbling stone to the disobedient.

options.text: They stumble because they disobey the word, as was appointed.
options.note: True to verse 8, though the vivid point is that Christ Himself becomes their stumbling stone.

options.text: They are simply left neutral, neither helped nor harmed.
options.note: There is no neutral ground; the stone either saves or trips.

options.text: They are honored for their independence.
options.note: Rejecting the stone leads to stumbling, not honor.

quiz.q: Which titles does Peter give the church in verse 9?

options.text: A chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God's own possession.
options.note: Verse 9 lifts these covenant titles from Exodus and Isaiah and applies them to the church.

options.text: A gathered crowd waiting for future status.
options.note: The titles describe a present, God-given identity, not a hoped-for one.

options.text: A priestly and holy people belonging to God.
options.note: True in substance, though the answer should name the full cascade of titles.

options.text: Honored citizens of the Roman world.
options.note: Their dignity is covenantal before God, not social standing in the empire.

quiz.q: For what purpose has God made them His chosen people?

options.text: To proclaim the praises of Him who called them out of darkness into His marvelous light.
options.note: Verse 9: election is for the mission of declaring God's praise.

options.text: To enjoy a privileged status apart from any calling.
options.note: The status carries a purpose: to proclaim God's praises.

options.text: To live for their own comfort and security.
options.note: They are chosen for God's praise and mission, not merely for comfort.

options.text: To shine God's light and mercy before the world.
options.note: True and close, though Peter names proclaiming His praises as the stated purpose.

questions: Peter lists sins to rid yourselves of: malice, deceit, hypocrisy, envy, slander. Which of these still clings to you, and what would it mean to put it off?
Do you crave God's word the way a hungry newborn craves milk? What dulls your appetite, and how might you recover it?
Christ is precious to those who believe but a stumbling stone to those who reject Him. How have you seen the same Jesus divide people in your own experience?
You are called a chosen people and a royal priesthood, however the world sees you. How would truly believing this change the way you carry yourself this week?
You were called out of darkness into marvelous light to proclaim God's praises. Who in your life might God be inviting you to declare His goodness to?

translationNotes.note: Pure spiritual milk renders logikon gala; the word can suggest reasonable or word-related milk, closely tied to the enduring word of God just mentioned in 1:23 to 25.
Peter weaves three stone texts, Isaiah 28:16, Psalm 118:22, and Isaiah 8:14, into a single portrait of Christ as cornerstone and stumbling stone.
The titles chosen people, royal priesthood, holy nation, and people for God's possession are drawn from Exodus 19:5 to 6 and Isaiah 43:20 to 21, applied now to the church.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Peter 2:1-10. The em dash in verse 8 was rendered as a comma per the no-dash rule, preserving wording.
